narrative_blocks.header: First wife died thirty days after wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Bishop's first wife Louisa Walter married him on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 — about thirty days later, at age nineteen. Timothy was twenty-three at the time. He went on to remarry twice and live another seventy-three years, dying at age ninety-five.

Timothy Bishop was a successful New Haven merchant and member of the Congregational Church. He served as a bank director at age ninety-one — still active in business governance four years before his death. The late-life professional engagement was characteristic of a Connecticut merchant patriarch who outlived three wives.

narrative_blocks.header: First wife died thirty days after wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Bishop's first wife Louisa Walter married him on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 — about thirty days later, at age nineteen. Timothy was twenty-three at the time. He went on to remarry twice and live another seventy-three years, dying at age ninety-five.

Timothy Bishop was a successful New Haven merchant and member of the Congregational Church. He served as a bank director at age ninety-one — still active in business governance four years before his death. The late-life professional engagement was characteristic of a Connecticut merchant patriarch who outlived three wives.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty days after her April marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 at age nineteen — about thirty days after the marriage, with no children. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month was striking even by the period's standards of vulnerability to acute disease. Her husband Timothy lived another seventy-three years and remarried twice.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa is buried at Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven, Plot 11 Cypress Avenue East. The cemetery — one of America's first planned burial grounds and a National Historic Landmark — became the family-burial cluster for the Walter-Pierpont line, with her parents William Jay Walter and Theodosia and several siblings later joining her there.
